case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for the adsorbate with SMILES [N]=O on the provided surface.

plan.reasoning: No prior attempts exist, so I should propose the best initial high-probability adsorption motif rather than terminate. The user wants the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for NO on a Pb/Y alloy surface, so the strategy should begin with the chemically most plausible strong-binding mode. SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=O, i.e. nitric oxide. The provided atom list shows N at index 0 with SP2 hybridization and 1 radical electron, and O at index 1 with SP2 hybridization and 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between the SMILES and the intended species. Because I plan to bind through N as a radical center, the Radical check is satisfied: index 0 does have an unpaired electron. Therefore adsorbate_type should be ReactiveSpecies, not Molecule. For NO on metal/alloy surfaces, N-end adsorption is typically more favorable than O-end adsorption because the singly occupied orbital and stronger interaction are centered more on N; bridge and hollow motifs can also be relevant, but as an initial systematic search the most chemically reasonable starting point is N-down ontop adsorption. On a heterogeneous Pb/Y surface, the more electropositive/reactive Y site is the best first guess for strongest chemisorption compared with Pb. Available ontop sites include Pb and Y, and site naming must use the canonical label ontop. Thus I choose ontop on Y with adsorbate binding through N index 0. This is a valid single-point adsorption plan and respects all constraints.

attempt_records.history_entry: 【✅ Perfect Adsorption】 ontop @ ['Y'] (Index [0]) -> Site: hollow (FCC/No-Subsurf) (Pb,Pb,Y) [🌟 New Best] | E=-2.716 eV

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: the adsorbate is [N]=O, i.e. nitric oxide. The provided atom list is consistent with this: N index 0 is SP2 with 1 radical electron, O index 1 is SP2 with 0 radical electrons. There is no mismatch between requested species and SMILES. Because I plan to bind through N and N indeed has an unpaired electron, classifying this as ReactiveSpecies is chemically valid. History analysis: only one successful plan exists so far: initial ontop on Y using adsorbate atom 0, which relaxed to a hollow Pb-Pb-Y site and gave the current best energy of -2.716 eV. No [🔄 Converged to known best] termination tag is present, so termination is not yet required. However, the prior result strongly suggests the NO radical prefers N-down coordination in a multi-center environment involving Pb and Y rather than remaining on isolated Y ontop. To systematically search for a lower energy configuration, the most chemically distinct untested high-symmetry option is to start directly at a bridge site. Among available bridges, Pb-Y is especially promising on this alloy because the current best hollow already includes both Pb and Y, indicating heterobimetallic coordination is favorable. User intent is to find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for NO on this Pb-Y surface, so the next plan should target a new, physically plausible high-symmetry site not yet attempted. Adsorbate analysis: NO is a diatomic radical; N is the preferred anchoring atom on many metal surfaces due to the radical character and strong donation/back-donation interactions. O (index 1) has no radical electrons, so it should not be used as the binding atom for a ReactiveSpecies plan. Surface analysis: allowed sites are ontop (Pb, Y), bridge (Pb-Pb, Pb-Y), hollow (Pb-Pb-Pb, Pb-Pb-Y). Since the previous Y-ontop start already collapsed into hollow Pb-Pb-Y, a direct hollow Pb-Pb-Y plan risks reproducing the known minimum. A bridge Pb-Y start is a distinct and systematic next test that can reveal whether a lower-energy tilted N-bound state exists or whether it funnels back to the same hollow geometry.
